chain_of_thought_evaluation.Q1_analysis: The paper explicitly states: "Our full FIRestarter prototype implementation is open source and available at https://github.com/vusec/firestarter." This provides a working link to the code repository.
chain_of_thought_evaluation.Q2_analysis: The evaluation uses real-world applications (Nginx, Apache, Lighttpd) running their standard test suite workloads on physical hardware (Intel i7-6700K). Furthermore, it involves instrumentation and testing against production bugs. This qualifies as using physical testbeds/real deployments.
chain_of_thought_evaluation.Q3_analysis: The paper has a dedicated section titled 'II. FAULT MODEL' where it explicitly states: "With our focus on survivability of applications, we assume a fail-stop fault model (even for persistent hardware faults) like most of the research in the area [19]-[21]..." This is a clear and formal definition.
chain_of_thought_evaluation.Q4_analysis: The paper provides rigorous, data-driven analysis of trade-offs. It quantifies performance overheads (e.g., "low performance overhead of at most 17%" on Nginx) and analyzes the impact of different adaptive policies on throughput degradation (Figure 3), comparing FIRestarter against STM-only and HTM-only approaches.

major_weakness: The paper is exceptionally strong across all metrics; the lowest score was tied at Q1 (if we consider that providing a link to code/data is sufficient for 2 points, which it is).
